items.title: In a life marked by tragedy, Martin Short still chooses joy
items.summary_auto: If there's a thin line separating laughter and pain, then a new Netflix documentary out Tuesday reveals Martin Short's walked that line for most of his life.

items.title: Jailed Iranian Nobel laureate's health condition 'very dangerous,' husband says
items.summary_auto: The family of Iranian Nobel Peace laureate Narges Mohammadi say she remains in critical care after she was transferred to a Tehran hospital as a result of "intensified international pressure," over her deteriorating health condition.

items.title: ICC opens arrest warrant for Philippines senator involved in Duterte's deadly war on drugs
items.summary_auto: The International Criminal Court unsealed an arrest warrant for a prominent Philippines senator linked to the deadly war on drugs overseen by ex-president Rodrigo Duterte, which allegedly involved the extrajudicial killings of suspects.

items.title: Man who killed star Olympic swimmer charged in multimillion-dollar grandparent scam
items.summary_auto: U.S. prosecutors have accused Glen Crossley, the 56-year-old Montrealer who killed a Canadian Olympic gold medallist in a hit-and-run, of scamming elderly Americans as part of a multimillion-dollar grandparent scam network.

items.title: Census 2026 is here. Why does it ask what time you leave for work?
items.summary_auto: Canadians are once again being asked detailed questions in this year’s census. What is Statistics Canada trying to learn from it all?

items.title: Québécois dubbing of The Simpsons saved after outcry from fans
items.summary_auto: The version of The Simpsons dubbed in Québécois French has been saved after Bell Media reached a deal with Disney Entertainment to broadcast the series.

items.title: Manitoba hockey coach who lured, sexually assaulted teenage player gets 6 years in prison
items.summary_auto: Madison Biluk, a former Manitoba hockey coach who admitted to luring and sexually assaulting a teenage player on her team, was taken into custody Monday to begin serving a six-year prison sentence.

items.title: IN PHOTOS | A tulip showcase in Canada's capital
items.summary_auto: The colourful tulips at Commissioners Park in Ottawa are on full display during the Canadian Tulip Festival, which runs May 8-18.

items.title: Ohio man charged with online luring of 10-year-old Manitoba girl
items.summary_auto: A 32-year-old Ohio man has been charged after a 10-year-old Manitoba girl was lured into sending sexually explicit images and videos to someone she thought was a teenage boy, RCMP say.

items.title: Dua Lipa sues Samsung for $15M, alleging unauthorized use of her image
items.summary_auto: ‌British pop star Dua Lipa has filed a lawsuit against Samsung Electronics seeking at least $15 million US in damages, accusing the South Korean tech giant of using her image without permission to market its television sets.

items.title: Scientists are warning Canadians to get ready for a U.S. tick invasion this year
items.summary_auto: Scientists say new species of ticks carrying troubling pathogens travelling our way from the northern United States at a rate of about 50 kilometres per year, including the lone star tick which can leave a bitten person with a severe allergy to meat.

items.title: Man accused in White House press gallery shooting pleads not guilty
items.summary_auto: A man accused of storming the White House Correspondents’ Association dinner while armed with guns and knives pleaded not guilty on Monday to charges that he attempted to kill U.S. President Donald Trump and fired a shotgun at a Secret Service officer who tried to stop the attack.

items.title: End of ceasefire deadline looms as Russia, Ukraine blame each other for continued attacks
items.summary_auto: A U.S.-brokered ceasefire between Russia and Ukraine was due to expire Monday with both sides accusing each other of breaching the 72-hour arrangement, as American and European officials considered how they might steer the warring countries into further talks.

items.title: Garter snakes emerge late from Narcisse dens after cool Manitoba spring
items.summary_auto: The largest gathering of snakes in the world is now underway in Manitoba — a little later than usual — as tens of thousands of garter snakes emerge from their dens in Narcisse.

items.title: Alberta's privacy watchdog concerned incoming law allows Crown to sell customers' personal information
items.summary_auto: Alberta’s information and privacy commissioner says newly passed legislation allowing a Crown corporation to sell users’ personal information to a private company sets a “concerning precedent.”

items.title: B.C. daycare worker dismayed by changes to provincial nominee program, warns of worker shortages
items.summary_auto: A daycare worker in Nanaimo, B.C., is speaking out against recent changes made to the provincial nominee program that now exclude her job from a possible pathway to permanent residency.

items.title: U.S. hybrid-car sales soar, along with gas prices
items.summary_auto: Hybrid ales up 37 per cent since ​Iran conflict, while EV sales lag, unlike strong gains in Europe. Pick-up truck sales remain strong.

items.title: 'Storms are getting bigger': N.L. fishers celebrate new funding for harbour upgrades
items.summary_auto: Fishery workers in Newfoundland and Labrador are celebrating new federal funding for small craft harbours, with some saying wharf and breakwater upgrades are critical to keep up with worsening storms.

items.title: How N.S. municipalities are preparing for drought and dry wells
items.summary_auto: There is currently a patchwork approach to drought across the province, with some municipalities taking action, and others yet to start conversations about long-term plans.

items.title: Farmers confront rising cost of fertilizer and fuel as spring seeding underway
I left home to prove I was independent. Coming back was braver
items.summary_auto: When Jumol Royes left home for school in Ottawa in midlife, he sought independence. But being there taught him just how much he relied on the community he had back home.

items.title: WestJet accused of tricky manoeuvre to deny dozens of passengers compensation after flight cancellations
items.summary_auto: Go Public has learned of more than 30 passengers who were denied compensation after WestJet cancelled flights and blamed safety-related maintenance. Flight data suggests the airline swapped in aircraft requiring maintenance shortly before those cancellations — a practice the regulator called out four years ago.

items.title: Assault, harassment charges against Ottawa police officer stem from alleged intimate partner violence
items.summary_auto: CBC News has learned that recent criminal charges against an Ottawa Police Service officer relate to alleged intimate partner violence and involve a police-issued Glock pistol.

items.title: Guelph, Ont., student's squirrel obsession earns her spot at Canada-wide science competition
items.summary_auto: Kira Egete and her family are obsessed with squirrels. Turns out, it's not such a nutty passion. Now, the Grade 10 student, whose project examines how squirrels see colour, will be among 400 participants at the Canada-Wide Science Fair in Edmonton later this month.
